3→

Nudge - AI Agent for SRE Engineers 🔥

4→
5→ 6→Nudge worfklow diagram: 7→https://thd.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DBRE/pages/2793472113/DBRE_Nudge 8→ 9→![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b02b9ad5-1599-4e03-8cb0-c8abbf6d083b) 10→ 11→ 12→ 13→Improve developer experience and reduce mean-time-to-respond (MTTR) by transforming alerts from this 👇 14→ 15→![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/16522dc3-6c2c-467f-9a9b-265af638833e) 16→ 17→To this 👇 18→ 19→![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2547de9b-2e4b-451c-bf8a-3aa45af7981f) 20→ 21→And you can ask addiitonal details: 22→![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1e7d4bf3-2160-4c2a-aebb-d9c8dbdf7505) 23→ 24→ 25→### Key Features 26→- **Automatic data collection:** Nudge will collect all available data about failed deployments from multiple clusters and multiple logging, monitoring, and tracing tools 27→- **Runbook automation and knowledge sharing:** Tell Nudge how you investigate today and it will automate it 28→- **Extensible:** Add your own data sources like Loki, Prometheus, Tempo, Helm, Kubernetes, etc. and will use them to investigate 29→- **Data Privacy:** Bring your own API key for any AI provider (OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, etc) 30→ 31→## Ways to Use Nudge 32→ 33→- Root cause analysis for Prometheus/AlertManager alerts in Slack 34→- live conversation with Nudge in Slack asking more details for the further invsetigation 35→- Query observability data in human language, e.g. `@Nudge provide me with the list of pods in crashloopbackoff in my cluster and why?` 36→- Choose LLM provider (OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, etc) 37→ 38→ 39→## Installation 40→ 41→**Prerequisite:** Get an API key for a supported LLM. 42→ 43→**Installation Methods:** 44→ 45→
46→ 47→Configuration for local development 48→ 49→First [install poetry (the python package manager)](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installing-with-the-official-installer) 50→ 51→- [Prometheus](https://thehomedepot.grafana.net/explore?schemaVersion=1&panes=%7B%22a8r%22%3A%7B%22datasource%22%3A%22HniOlTlnz%22%2C%22queries%22%3A%5B%7B%22refId%22%3A%22A%22%2C%22expr%22%3A%22%22%2C%22range%22%3Atrue%2C%22instant%22%3Atrue%2C%22datasource%22%3A%7B%22type%22%3A%22prometheus%22%2C%22uid%22%3A%22HniOlTlnz%22%7D%7D%5D%2C%22range%22%3A%7B%22from%22%3A%22now-1h%22%2C%22to%22%3A%22now%22%7D%7D%7D&orgId=1) 52→- [Slack THD Nudge app](https://api.slack.com/apps/A08FGUJK7ND/general) 53→- [THD Nudge Deployment](https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/tree/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes) 54→- [THD OTEL Demo Deployment](https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/otel-demo/app.ts) 55→ 56→```bash 57→git clone https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s.git 58→cd com-cdk8s 59→make synth spinnaker argus team=com-dbre; 60→``` 61→ 62→```bash 63→export ALLOWED_TOOLSETS="network,internet,loki/loki,tempo/tempo,kubernetes/core,prometheus" # allows to control which toolsets are allowed to be used by the agent 64→export API_KEY="" # required for all LLMs, check out the LLM provider documentation 65→export GRAFANA_LOKI_DATASOURCE_UID="" 66→export GRAFANA_PROMETHEUS_DATASOURCE_UID="" 67→export GRAFANA_TEMPO_DATASOURCE_UID="" 68→export GRAFANA_TOKEN="" 69→export GRAFANA_URL=https://thehomedepot.grafana.net 70→export HOLMES_SEND_COSTS=True # if you want to see the cost of the request 71→export LOG_LEVEL=INFO # INFO, DEBUG, WARNING, ERROR are available 72→export MODEL="vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash" # or any other model that supports agent tools 73→export MODEL_REASONING_EFFORT= # in case if you use a model that supports reasoning effort 74→export MODEL_TEMPERATURE=0 # in case if you use a model that supports temperature 75→export SLACK_APP_TOKEN="< Slack App Token from this THD app https://api.slack.com/apps/A08FGUJK7ND/general>, required for bi-directional communication with Nudge" 76→export SLACK_CHANNEL=", e.g. 'Nudge-dev'" 77→export SLACK_DB_ENABLED=False # if you want to enable storing conversations with the app. It will be stored in the local sqlite database 78→export SLACK_TOKEN="" 79→export TEMPO_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 80→export LOKI_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 81→export PROMETHEUS_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 82→# Setting up postges database - if unset, defaults are used, which match local postgres from docker-compose 83→export PG_PASSWORD="" 84→export PG_USER="" 85→export PG_HOST="" 86→export PG_PORT="" 87→export PG_DATABASE="" 88→# Connecting Nudge to MCP server. 89→export MCP_JSON="{}" # see example below for configuring. 90→``` 91→
92→ 93→
94→ 95→From Source (Python Poetry) 96→ 97→First [install poetry (the python package manager)](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installing-with-the-official-installer) 98→ 99→``` 100→git clone https://github.com/one-thd/thd-holmes.git 101→cd thd-holmes 102→poetry install --no-root 103→``` 104→
105→ 106→
107→From Source (Docker) 108→ 109→Clone the project from github, and then run: 110→ 111→```bash 112→cd thd-holmes 113→docker build -t holmes . 114→docker run -it --net=host -v -v ~/.holmes:/root/.holmes -v ~/.aws:/root/.aws -v ~/.config/gcloud:/root/.config/gcloud -v $HOME/.kube/config:/root/.kube/config holmes ask "what pods are unhealthy and why?" 115→``` 116→
117→ 118→
119→Run Nudge in your cluster (Helm) 120→ 121→Using commonly adopted THD K8s CDK: 122→https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes/app.ts 123→In this mode, all the parameters should be passed to the Nudge deployment, using environment variables. 124→ 125→Make sure you've configured AlertManger routing: 126→https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes/app.ts#L215 127→ 128→
129→ 130→### Getting an API Key 131→ 132→Nudge requires an LLM API Key to function. The most common option is OpenAI, but many [LiteLLM-compatible](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/providers/) models are supported. To use an LLM, set `--model` (e.g. `gpt-4o` or `bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0`) and `--api-key` (if necessary). Depending on the provider, you may need to set environment variables too. 133→ 134→ 135→### Enabling Integrations 136→ 137→
138→Confluence 139→Nudge can read runbooks from Confluence. To give it access, set the following environment variables: 140→ 141→* CONFLUENCE_BASE_URL - e.g. https://robusta-dev-test.atlassian.net 142→* CONFLUENCE_USER - e.g. user@company.com 143→* CONFLUENCE_API_KEY - [refer to Atlassian docs on generating API keys](https://support.atlassian.com/atlassian-account/docs/manage-api-tokens-for-your-atlassian-account/) 144→
145→ 146→
147→ 148→Jira, GitHub, OpsGenie, PagerDuty, and AlertManager 149→ 150→ 151→Nudge can pull tickets/alerts from each of these sources and investigate them. 152→ 153→Refer to `holmes investigate jira --help` etc for details. 154→
155→ 156→
157→Find the right configuration to change in big Helm charts 158→ 159→LLM uses the built-in [Helm toolset](./holmes/plugins/toolsets/helm.yaml) to gather information. 160→
161→ 162→## Customizing Nudge 163→ 164→### How to Add a New Tool 165→ 166→Adding a new tool to your AI agent can be done in two ways: using YAML configuration files for CLI tools or implementing Python classes for more complex tools. Here's a step-by-step guide for both approaches. 167→ 168→#### Option 1: Adding a CLI Tool via YAML 169→ 170→CLI tools are defined in YAML files and are suitable for simple command-line operations. They should be defined in they YAML file in the holmes/plugins/toolsets folder 171→ 172→**Steps:** 173→ 174→1. Create or edit a YAML file with your toolset definition 175→2. Define the toolset with any prerequisites 176→3. Add your tool definitions within the toolset 177→4. In config.py append your toolset name to the allowed_toolsets string 178→5. Nudge can investigate many issues out of the box, with no customization or training. 179→ 180→**Example** 181→```yaml 182→toolsets: 183→ - name: "my-toolset/my-tool" 184→ prerequisites: 185→ - command: "command-to-check-availability" # Optional 186→ 187→ tools: 188→ - name: "my_new_tool" 189→ description: "Description of what the tool does" 190→ command: "actual-command --with {{ parameter1 }} {% if parameter2 %} --optional {{ parameter2 }}{% endif %}" 191→``` 192→The template syntax ({{ }} and {% %}) allows for parameter substitution and conditional logic in your commands. 193→ 194→#### Option 2: Adding a Python Tool 195→For more complex tools requiring additional logic, you'll need to implement the Tool abstract class. 196→ 197→**Steps:** 198→1. Create a new Python class that inherits from the Tool abstract class 199→2. Define the tool's properties (name, description, parameters) 200→3. Implement the required abstract methods: 201→4. invoke: Executes the tool with given parameters 202→5. **get_parameterized_one_liner**: Returns a string representation of the tool invocation 203→6. Add your tool to a Toolset class 204→7. In the **toolsets/__init__.p**y add your toolset to the return list in **load_python_toolsets** function 205→ 206→
207→ 208→Tool definition example: 209→ 210→ 211→```python 212→from typing import Any 213→ 214→class MyNewTool(Tool): 215→ def __init__(self): 216→ super().__init__( 217→ name="my_new_tool", 218→ description="Description of what my tool does and when to use it", 219→ parameters={ 220→ "param1": ToolParameter( 221→ description="Description of parameter 1", 222→ type="string", 223→ required=True, 224→ ), 225→ "param2": ToolParameter( 226→ description="Description of parameter 2", 227→ type="string", 228→ required=False, 229→ ), 230→ }, 231→ ) 232→ 233→ def invoke(self, params: Any) -> str: 234→ # Extract parameters 235→ param1: str = params["param1"] 236→ param2: str = params.get("param2", "default_value") 237→ 238→ # Your tool's logic here 239→ result = some_function(param1, param2) 240→ 241→ return result 242→ 243→ def get_parameterized_one_liner(self, params) -> str: 244→ # Return a string representation of this tool invocation 245→ param1: str = params["param1"] 246→ return f"my-tool-{param1}-execution.txt" 247→``` 248→
249→ 250→
251→ 252→Register the Tool in a Toolset: 253→ 254→ 255→```python 256→class MyToolset(Toolset): 257→ def __init__(self): 258→ super().__init__( 259→ name="my/toolset", 260→ prerequisites=[ 261→ ToolsetEnvironmentPrerequisite( 262→ env=["REQUIRED_ENV_VAR1", "REQUIRED_ENV_VAR2"] 263→ ) 264→ ], 265→ tools=[MyNewTool()], 266→ ) 267→ self.check_prerequisites() 268→``` 269→
270→ 271→Best Practices 272→ 273→1. **Tool Naming**: Use clear, descriptive names following snake_case convention 274→2. **Descriptions**: Provide comprehensive descriptions of what the tool does and when to use it 275→3. **Parameters**: Document each parameter thoroughly with clear descriptions 276→4. **Error Handling**: Implement proper error handling in your invoke method 277→5. **Testing**: Test your tool thoroughly before deployment 278→6. **Prerequisites**: Clearly define any prerequisites needed for your tool to function 279→7. **Prompt tuning**: Additionally, it might help adding how the tool fits in the investigation workflow to the holmes/plugins/prompts/generic_investigation.jinja2 template 280→ 281→By following these guidelines, you can successfully add new tools to extend your AI agent's capabilities. 282→### How to Change the LLM Used by the Agent 283→Our AI agent uses LiteLLM to provide a unified interface to different language models across providers. This makes it straightforward to switch between models like OpenAI's GPT, Google's Gemini, or Anthropic's Claude. Here's how to change the underlying LLM for our agent. 284→ 285→#### Basic Configuration via Environment Variables 286→The simplest way to change the model is by setting environment variables: 287→```bash 288→# Set the model to use 289→export MODEL="openai/gpt-4o" 290→ 291→# Optional: Configure model parameters 292→export MODEL_MAX_STEPS=15 293→export MODEL_TEMPERATURE=0.01 294→``` 295→The core settings are defined in Holmes/common/env_vars.py: 296→```bash 297→MODEL = os.environ.get('MODEL', 'vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash') # Default model 298→MODEL_MAX_STEPS = os.environ.get('MODEL_MAX_STEPS', 15) 299→MODEL_TEMPERATURE = os.environ.get('MODEL_TEMPERATURE', 0.01) 300→``` 301→#### Important Model Parameter Considerations 302→ 303→**Temperature vs. Reasoning Effort** 304→ 305→**Regular Models**: Use **MODEL_TEMPERATURE** (usually between 0-1) 306→For deterministic results, keep temperature below 0.2 307→Lower temperature = more predictable responses 308→**Reasoning Models**: Use **reasoning_effort** instead of temperature 309→Valid values: "low", "medium", "high" 310→Example: ```export REASONING_EFFORT="high"``` 311→ 312→**Note**: Setting the wrong parameter type for a model may cause errors or be ignored silently 313→ 314→#### Supported Models 315→LiteLLM uses the format provider/model-name. For example: 316→ 317→* openai/gpt-01 318→* gemini/gemini-2.0-pro 319→* anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229 320→ 321→You can see all supported providers, models, and configurations on the official website. 322→ 323→**Important:** used model should support tool calls. To verify it consult with the aforementioned website or official model documentation. 324→ 325→ 326→#### Setting API Keys 327→Each provider requires its own API key, which must be set as an environment variable: 328→```bash 329→# For OpenAI 330→export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..." 331→# For Google Gemini 332→export GOOGLE_API_KEY="AIza..." 333→# For Anthropic 334→export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..." 335→``` 336→#### Trying Unlisted Models 337→**Important:** The official LiteLLM documentation may not immediately reflect newly released models. If a model is not explicitly listed in the documentation, it might still be supported. 338→ 339→To try a newly released model: 340→ 341→1. Follow the naming convention (provider/model-name) 342→2. Set the appropriate API key 343→3. Run a simple test to verify it works 344→For example, if a new model "super-model" was just released by OpenAI: 345→```bash 346→export MODEL="openai/super-model" 347→export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key" 348→``` 349→#### Testing After Changing Models 350→After changing the model, it's recommended to run a simple test to verify the integration is working correctly: 351→```bash 352→# Example script to test model connectivity 353→python -c "import os; from litellm import completion; \ 354→print(completion(model=os.environ.get('MODEL'), \ 355→messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello, are you working?'}]))" 356→``` 357→ 358→
359→Add Custom Runbooks 360→ 361→Nudge can investigate by following runbooks written in plain English. Add your own runbooks to provided the LLM specific instructions. 362→ 363→New runbooks are loaded using `-r` from [custom runbook files](./examples/custom_runbooks.yaml) or by adding them to the `~/.holmes/config.yaml` with the `custom_runbooks: ["/path/to/runbook.yaml"]`. 364→
365→ 366→
367→Reading settings from a config file 368→ 369→You can customize Nudge's behaviour with command line flags, or you can save common settings in config file for re-use. 370→ 371→You can view an example config file with all available settings [here](config.example.yaml). 372→ 373→By default, without specifying `--config` the agent will try to read `~/.holmes/config.yaml`. When settings are present in both config file and cli, the cli option takes precedence. 374→ 375→
376→Custom Toolsets 377→ 378→You can define your own custom toolsets to extend the functionality of your setup. These toolsets can include querying company-specific data, fetching logs from observability tools, and more. 379→ 380→```bash 381→# Add paths to your custom toolsets here 382→# Example: ["path/to/your/custom_toolset.yaml"] 383→#custom_toolsets: ["examples/custom_toolset.yaml"] 384→``` 385→
386→ 387→
388→ 389→Alertmanager Configuration 390→ 391→Configure the URL for your Alertmanager instance to enable alert management and notifications. 392→ 393→```bash 394→# URL for the Alertmanager 395→#alertmanager_url: "http://localhost:9093" 396→``` 397→
398→ 399→
400→ 401→Jira Integration 402→ 403→Integrate with Jira to automate issue tracking and project management tasks. Provide your Jira credentials and specify the query to fetch issues and optionally update their status. 404→ 405→```bash 406→# Jira credentials and query settings 407→#jira_username: "user@company.com" 408→#jira_api_key: "..." 409→#jira_url: "https://your-company.atlassian.net" 410→#jira_query: "project = 'Natan Test Project' and Status = 'To Do'" 411→``` 412→ 413→1. **jira_username**: The email you use to log into your Jira account. Eg: `jira-user@company.com` 414→2. **jira_api_key**: Follow these [instructions](https://support.atlassian.com/atlassian-account/docs/manage-api-tokens-for-your-atlassian-account/) to get your API key. 415→3. **jira_url**: The URL of your workspace. For example: [https://workspace.atlassian.net](https://workspace.atlassian.net) (**Note:** schema (https) is required) 416→4. **project**: Name of the project you want the Jira tickets to be created in. Go to **Project Settings** -> **Details** -> **Name**. 417→5. **status**: Status of a ticket. Example: `To Do`, `In Progress` 418→
419→ 420→
421→ 422→GitHub Integration 423→ 424→Integrate with GitHub to automate issue tracking and project management tasks. Provide your GitHub PAT (*personal access token*) and specify the `owner/repository`. 425→ 426→```bash 427→# GitHub credentials and query settings 428→#github_owner: "robusta-dev" 429→#github_pat: "..." 430→#github_url: "https://api.github.com" (default) 431→#github_repository: "Nudge" 432→#github_query: "is:issue is:open" 433→``` 434→ 435→1. **github_owner**: The repository owner. Eg: `robusta-dev` 436→2. **github_pat**: Follow these [instructions](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/managing-your-personal-access-tokens#creating-a-fine-grained-personal-access-token) to get your GitHub pat (*personal access token*). 437→3. **github_url**: The URL of your GitHub API. For example: [https://api.github.com](https://api.github.com) (**Note:** schema (https) is required) 438→4. **github_repository**: Name of the repository you want the GitHub issues to be scanned. Eg: `Nudge`. 439→
440→ 441→
442→PagerDuty Integration 443→ 444→Integrate with PagerDuty to automate incident tracking and project management tasks. Provide your PagerDuty credentials and specify the user email to update the incident with findings. 445→ 446→```bash 447→pagerduty_api_key: "..." 448→pagerduty_user_email: "user@mail.com" 449→pagerduty_incident_key: "..." 450→``` 451→ 452→1. **pagerduty_api_key**: The PagerDuty API key. This can be found in the PagerDuty UI under Integrations > API Access Key. 453→2. **pagerduty_user_email**: When --update is set, which user will be listed as the user who updated the incident. (Must be the email of a valid user in your PagerDuty account.) 454→3. **pagerduty_incident_key**: If provided, only analyze a single PagerDuty incident matching this key 455→
456→ 457→
458→OpsGenie Integration 459→ 460→Integrate with OpsGenie to automate alert investigations. Provide your OpsGenie credentials and specify the query to fetch alerts. 461→ 462→```bash 463→opsgenie_api_key : "..." 464→opsgenie-team-integration-key: "...." 465→opsgenie-query: "..." 466→``` 467→ 468→1. **opsgenie_api_key**: The OpsGenie API key. Get it from Settings > API key management > Add new API key 469→2. **opsgenie-team-integration-key**: OpsGenie Team Integration key for writing back results. (NOT a normal API Key.) Get it from Teams > YourTeamName > Integrations > Add Integration > API Key. Don't forget to turn on the integration and add the Team as Responders to the alert. 470→3. **opsgenie-query**: E.g. 'message: Foo' (see https://support.atlassian.com/opsgenie/docs/search-queries-for-alerts/) 471→
472→ 473→ 474→
475→ 476→Slack Integration 477→ 478→Configure Slack to send notifications to specific channels. Provide your Slack token and the desired channel for notifications. 479→ 480→```bash 481→# Slack token and channel configuration 482→#slack_token: "..." 483→#slack_channel: "#general" 484→``` 485→ 486→1. **slack-token**: The Slack API key. You can generate with `pip install robusta-cli && robusta integrations slack` 487→2. **slack-channel**: The Slack channel where you want to receive the findings. 488→ 489→
490→ 491→
492→ 493→Custom Runbooks 494→ 495→Define custom runbooks to give explicit instructions to the LLM on how to investigate certain alerts. This can help in achieving better results for known alerts. 496→ 497→```bash 498→# Add paths to your custom runbooks here 499→# Example: ["path/to/your/custom_runbook.yaml"] 500→#custom_runbooks: ["examples/custom_runbooks.yaml"] 501→``` 502→
503→ 504→### Large Language Model (LLM) Configuration 505→ 506→Choose between OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, and more. Provide the necessary API keys and endpoints for the selected service. 507→ 508→ 509→
510→ 511→OpenAI 512→ 513→```bash 514→# Configuration for OpenAI LLM 515→#api_key: "your-secret-api-key" 516→``` 517→
518→ 519→
520→ 521→Azure 522→ 523→```bash 524→# Configuration for Azure LLM 525→#api_key: "your-secret-api-key" 526→#model: "azure/" 527→#you will also need to set environment variables - see above 528→``` 529→
530→ 531→Bedrock 532→ 533→```bash 534→# Configuration for AWS Bedrock LLM 535→#model: "bedrock/" 536→#you will also need to set environment variables - see above 537→``` 538→
539→ 540→ 541→ 542→### Using Remote MCP Server 543→Nudge can integrate with remote MCP servers using streamable-http mode through MCP Proxy (when you have multiple remote servers) 544→or sse-mode for basic connection. 545→#### Example MCP configuration: 546→ 547→Data for MCP requires the following format: 548→Connecting Nudge to proxy: 549→``` 550→{ 551→ "mcpServers": { 552→ "proxy": { 553→ "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8080/mcp/", 554→ "headers": { 555→ "Authorization": "Bearer " 556→ }, 557→ 558→ } 559→ } 560→} 561→``` 562→Run 563→```bash 564→jq -c . | pbcopy 565→``` 566→and paste it to **MCP_JSON** environment variable. 567→ 568→Configuring Proxy: 569→``` 570→{ 571→ "mcpServers": { 572→ "kubernetes": { 573→ "url": "http://kubernetes-mcp-server/sse", 574→ "transport": "sse" 575→ } 576→ },... 577→} 578→``` 579→Adapt configuration in docker compose. 580→## License 581→ 582→Distributed under the MIT License. See [LICENSE.txt](https://github.com/one-thd/thd-holmes/blob/master/LICENSE.txt) for more information. 583→ 584→ 585→## How to Contribute 586→ 587→To contribute to Nudge, first follow the Installation instructions for **running Nudge from source using Poetry.** 588→ 589→
590→Adding new runbooks 591→ 592→You can contribute knowledge on solving common alerts and Nudge will use this knowledge to solve related issues. To do so, add a new file to [./holmes/plugins/runbooks](holmes/plugins/runbooks) - or edit an existing runbooks file in that same directory. 593→ 594→
595→ 596→
597→Adding new toolsets 598→ 599→You can add define new tools in YAML and Nudge will use those tools in it's investigation. To do so, add a new file to [./holmes/plugins/toolsets](holmes/plugins/toolsets) - or edit an existing toolsets file in that same directory. 600→ 601→Note: if you prefer to keep your tools private, you can store them locally and pass them to Nudge with the `-t` flag. However, please consider contributing your toolsets! At least one other community member will probably find them useful! 602→ 603→
604→ 605→
606→Modifying the default prompts (prompt engineering) 607→ 608→The default prompts for Nudge are located in [./holmes/plugins/prompts](holmes/plugins/prompts). file.content:
Solve alerts faster with an AI assistant

Nudge - AI Agent for SRE Engineers 🔥

Nudge worfklow diagram: https://thd.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DBRE/pages/2793472113/DBRE_Nudge ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b02b9ad5-1599-4e03-8cb0-c8abbf6d083b) Improve developer experience and reduce mean-time-to-respond (MTTR) by transforming alerts from this 👇 ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/16522dc3-6c2c-467f-9a9b-265af638833e) To this 👇 ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2547de9b-2e4b-451c-bf8a-3aa45af7981f) And you can ask addiitonal details: ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1e7d4bf3-2160-4c2a-aebb-d9c8dbdf7505) ### Key Features - **Automatic data collection:** Nudge will collect all available data about failed deployments from multiple clusters and multiple logging, monitoring, and tracing tools - **Runbook automation and knowledge sharing:** Tell Nudge how you investigate today and it will automate it - **Extensible:** Add your own data sources like Loki, Prometheus, Tempo, Helm, Kubernetes, etc. and will use them to investigate - **Data Privacy:** Bring your own API key for any AI provider (OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, etc) ## Ways to Use Nudge - Root cause analysis for Prometheus/AlertManager alerts in Slack - live conversation with Nudge in Slack asking more details for the further invsetigation - Query observability data in human language, e.g. `@Nudge provide me with the list of pods in crashloopbackoff in my cluster and why?` - Choose LLM provider (OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, etc) ## Installation **Prerequisite:** Get an API key for a supported LLM. **Installation Methods:**
Configuration for local development First [install poetry (the python package manager)](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installing-with-the-official-installer) - [Prometheus](https://thehomedepot.grafana.net/explore?schemaVersion=1&panes=%7B%22a8r%22%3A%7B%22datasource%22%3A%22HniOlTlnz%22%2C%22queries%22%3A%5B%7B%22refId%22%3A%22A%22%2C%22expr%22%3A%22%22%2C%22range%22%3Atrue%2C%22instant%22%3Atrue%2C%22datasource%22%3A%7B%22type%22%3A%22prometheus%22%2C%22uid%22%3A%22HniOlTlnz%22%7D%7D%5D%2C%22range%22%3A%7B%22from%22%3A%22now-1h%22%2C%22to%22%3A%22now%22%7D%7D%7D&orgId=1) - [Slack THD Nudge app](https://api.slack.com/apps/A08FGUJK7ND/general) - [THD Nudge Deployment](https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/tree/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes) - [THD OTEL Demo Deployment](https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/otel-demo/app.ts) ```bash git clone https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s.git cd com-cdk8s make synth spinnaker argus team=com-dbre; ``` ```bash export ALLOWED_TOOLSETS="network,internet,loki/loki,tempo/tempo,kubernetes/core,prometheus" # allows to control which toolsets are allowed to be used by the agent export API_KEY="" # required for all LLMs, check out the LLM provider documentation export GRAFANA_LOKI_DATASOURCE_UID="" export GRAFANA_PROMETHEUS_DATASOURCE_UID="" export GRAFANA_TEMPO_DATASOURCE_UID="" export GRAFANA_TOKEN="" export GRAFANA_URL=https://thehomedepot.grafana.net export HOLMES_SEND_COSTS=True # if you want to see the cost of the request export LOG_LEVEL=INFO # INFO, DEBUG, WARNING, ERROR are available export MODEL="vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash" # or any other model that supports agent tools export MODEL_REASONING_EFFORT= # in case if you use a model that supports reasoning effort export MODEL_TEMPERATURE=0 # in case if you use a model that supports temperature export SLACK_APP_TOKEN="< Slack App Token from this THD app https://api.slack.com/apps/A08FGUJK7ND/general>, required for bi-directional communication with Nudge" export SLACK_CHANNEL=", e.g. 'Nudge-dev'" export SLACK_DB_ENABLED=False # if you want to enable storing conversations with the app. It will be stored in the local sqlite database export SLACK_TOKEN="" export TEMPO_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 export LOKI_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 export PROMETHEUS_LOOKBACK_PERIOD_SECONDS=900 # Setting up postges database - if unset, defaults are used, which match local postgres from docker-compose export PG_PASSWORD="" export PG_USER="" export PG_HOST="" export PG_PORT="" export PG_DATABASE="" # Connecting Nudge to MCP server. export MCP_JSON="{}" # see example below for configuring. ```
From Source (Python Poetry) First [install poetry (the python package manager)](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installing-with-the-official-installer) ``` git clone https://github.com/one-thd/thd-holmes.git cd thd-holmes poetry install --no-root ```
From Source (Docker) Clone the project from github, and then run: ```bash cd thd-holmes docker build -t holmes . docker run -it --net=host -v -v ~/.holmes:/root/.holmes -v ~/.aws:/root/.aws -v ~/.config/gcloud:/root/.config/gcloud -v $HOME/.kube/config:/root/.kube/config holmes ask "what pods are unhealthy and why?" ```
Run Nudge in your cluster (Helm) Using commonly adopted THD K8s CDK: https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes/app.ts In this mode, all the parameters should be passed to the Nudge deployment, using environment variables. Make sure you've configured AlertManger routing: https://github.com/one-thd/com-cdk8s/blob/main/apps/com-dbre/holmes/np-com-internal-thd/np-com-internal-us-central1-gke-1/holmes/app.ts#L215
### Getting an API Key Nudge requires an LLM API Key to function. The most common option is OpenAI, but many [LiteLLM-compatible](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/providers/) models are supported. To use an LLM, set `--model` (e.g. `gpt-4o` or `bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0`) and `--api-key` (if necessary). Depending on the provider, you may need to set environment variables too. ### Enabling Integrations
Confluence Nudge can read runbooks from Confluence. To give it access, set the following environment variables: * CONFLUENCE_BASE_URL - e.g. https://robusta-dev-test.atlassian.net * CONFLUENCE_USER - e.g. user@company.com * CONFLUENCE_API_KEY - [refer to Atlassian docs on generating API keys](https://support.atlassian.com/atlassian-account/docs/manage-api-tokens-for-your-atlassian-account/)
Jira, GitHub, OpsGenie, PagerDuty, and AlertManager Nudge can pull tickets/alerts from each of these sources and investigate them. Refer to `holmes investigate jira --help` etc for details.
Find the right configuration to change in big Helm charts LLM uses the built-in [Helm toolset](./holmes/plugins/toolsets/helm.yaml) to gather information.
## Customizing Nudge ### How to Add a New Tool Adding a new tool to your AI agent can be done in two ways: using YAML configuration files for CLI tools or implementing Python classes for more complex tools. Here's a step-by-step guide for both approaches. #### Option 1: Adding a CLI Tool via YAML CLI tools are defined in YAML files and are suitable for simple command-line operations. They should be defined in they YAML file in the holmes/plugins/toolsets folder **Steps:** 1. Create or edit a YAML file with your toolset definition 2. Define the toolset with any prerequisites 3. Add your tool definitions within the toolset 4. In config.py append your toolset name to the allowed_toolsets string 5. Nudge can investigate many issues out of the box, with no customization or training. **Example** ```yaml toolsets: - name: "my-toolset/my-tool" prerequisites: - command: "command-to-check-availability" # Optional tools: - name: "my_new_tool" description: "Description of what the tool does" command: "actual-command --with {{ parameter1 }} {% if parameter2 %} --optional {{ parameter2 }}{% endif %}" ``` The template syntax ({{ }} and {% %}) allows for parameter substitution and conditional logic in your commands. #### Option 2: Adding a Python Tool For more complex tools requiring additional logic, you'll need to implement the Tool abstract class. **Steps:** 1. Create a new Python class that inherits from the Tool abstract class 2. Define the tool's properties (name, description, parameters) 3. Implement the required abstract methods: 4. invoke: Executes the tool with given parameters 5. **get_parameterized_one_liner**: Returns a string representation of the tool invocation 6. Add your tool to a Toolset class 7. In the **toolsets/__init__.p**y add your toolset to the return list in **load_python_toolsets** function
Tool definition example: ```python from typing import Any class MyNewTool(Tool): def __init__(self): super().__init__( name="my_new_tool", description="Description of what my tool does and when to use it", parameters={ "param1": ToolParameter( description="Description of parameter 1", type="string", required=True, ), "param2": ToolParameter( description="Description of parameter 2", type="string", required=False, ), }, ) def invoke(self, params: Any) -> str: # Extract parameters param1: str = params["param1"] param2: str = params.get("param2", "default_value") # Your tool's logic here result = some_function(param1, param2) return result def get_parameterized_one_liner(self, params) -> str: # Return a string representation of this tool invocation param1: str = params["param1"] return f"my-tool-{param1}-execution.txt" ```
Register the Tool in a Toolset: ```python class MyToolset(Toolset): def __init__(self): super().__init__( name="my/toolset", prerequisites=[ ToolsetEnvironmentPrerequisite( env=["REQUIRED_ENV_VAR1", "REQUIRED_ENV_VAR2"] ) ], tools=[MyNewTool()], ) self.check_prerequisites() ```
Best Practices 1. **Tool Naming**: Use clear, descriptive names following snake_case convention 2. **Descriptions**: Provide comprehensive descriptions of what the tool does and when to use it 3. **Parameters**: Document each parameter thoroughly with clear descriptions 4. **Error Handling**: Implement proper error handling in your invoke method 5. **Testing**: Test your tool thoroughly before deployment 6. **Prerequisites**: Clearly define any prerequisites needed for your tool to function 7. **Prompt tuning**: Additionally, it might help adding how the tool fits in the investigation workflow to the holmes/plugins/prompts/generic_investigation.jinja2 template By following these guidelines, you can successfully add new tools to extend your AI agent's capabilities. ### How to Change the LLM Used by the Agent Our AI agent uses LiteLLM to provide a unified interface to different language models across providers. This makes it straightforward to switch between models like OpenAI's GPT, Google's Gemini, or Anthropic's Claude. Here's how to change the underlying LLM for our agent. #### Basic Configuration via Environment Variables The simplest way to change the model is by setting environment variables: ```bash # Set the model to use export MODEL="openai/gpt-4o" # Optional: Configure model parameters export MODEL_MAX_STEPS=15 export MODEL_TEMPERATURE=0.01 ``` The core settings are defined in Holmes/common/env_vars.py: ```bash MODEL = os.environ.get('MODEL', 'vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash') # Default model MODEL_MAX_STEPS = os.environ.get('MODEL_MAX_STEPS', 15) MODEL_TEMPERATURE = os.environ.get('MODEL_TEMPERATURE', 0.01) ``` #### Important Model Parameter Considerations **Temperature vs. Reasoning Effort** **Regular Models**: Use **MODEL_TEMPERATURE** (usually between 0-1) For deterministic results, keep temperature below 0.2 Lower temperature = more predictable responses **Reasoning Models**: Use **reasoning_effort** instead of temperature Valid values: "low", "medium", "high" Example: ```export REASONING_EFFORT="high"``` **Note**: Setting the wrong parameter type for a model may cause errors or be ignored silently #### Supported Models LiteLLM uses the format provider/model-name. For example: * openai/gpt-01 * gemini/gemini-2.0-pro * anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229 You can see all supported providers, models, and configurations on the official website. **Important:** used model should support tool calls. To verify it consult with the aforementioned website or official model documentation. #### Setting API Keys Each provider requires its own API key, which must be set as an environment variable: ```bash # For OpenAI export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..." # For Google Gemini export GOOGLE_API_KEY="AIza..." # For Anthropic export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..." ``` #### Trying Unlisted Models **Important:** The official LiteLLM documentation may not immediately reflect newly released models. If a model is not explicitly listed in the documentation, it might still be supported. To try a newly released model: 1. Follow the naming convention (provider/model-name) 2. Set the appropriate API key 3. Run a simple test to verify it works For example, if a new model "super-model" was just released by OpenAI: ```bash export MODEL="openai/super-model" export OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key" ``` #### Testing After Changing Models After changing the model, it's recommended to run a simple test to verify the integration is working correctly: ```bash # Example script to test model connectivity python -c "import os; from litellm import completion; \ print(completion(model=os.environ.get('MODEL'), \ messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Hello, are you working?'}]))" ```
Add Custom Runbooks Nudge can investigate by following runbooks written in plain English. Add your own runbooks to provided the LLM specific instructions. New runbooks are loaded using `-r` from [custom runbook files](./examples/custom_runbooks.yaml) or by adding them to the `~/.holmes/config.yaml` with the `custom_runbooks: ["/path/to/runbook.yaml"]`.
Reading settings from a config file You can customize Nudge's behaviour with command line flags, or you can save common settings in config file for re-use. You can view an example config file with all available settings [here](config.example.yaml). By default, without specifying `--config` the agent will try to read `~/.holmes/config.yaml`. When settings are present in both config file and cli, the cli option takes precedence.
Custom Toolsets You can define your own custom toolsets to extend the functionality of your setup. These toolsets can include querying company-specific data, fetching logs from observability tools, and more. ```bash # Add paths to your custom toolsets here # Example: ["path/to/your/custom_toolset.yaml"] #custom_toolsets: ["examples/custom_toolset.yaml"] ```
Alertmanager Configuration Configure the URL for your Alertmanager instance to enable alert management and notifications. ```bash # URL for the Alertmanager #alertmanager_url: "http://localhost:9093" ```
Jira Integration Integrate with Jira to automate issue tracking and project management tasks. Provide your Jira credentials and specify the query to fetch issues and optionally update their status. ```bash # Jira credentials and query settings #jira_username: "user@company.com" #jira_api_key: "..." #jira_url: "https://your-company.atlassian.net" #jira_query: "project = 'Natan Test Project' and Status = 'To Do'" ``` 1. **jira_username**: The email you use to log into your Jira account. Eg: `jira-user@company.com` 2. **jira_api_key**: Follow these [instructions](https://support.atlassian.com/atlassian-account/docs/manage-api-tokens-for-your-atlassian-account/) to get your API key. 3. **jira_url**: The URL of your workspace. For example: [https://workspace.atlassian.net](https://workspace.atlassian.net) (**Note:** schema (https) is required) 4. **project**: Name of the project you want the Jira tickets to be created in. Go to **Project Settings** -> **Details** -> **Name**. 5. **status**: Status of a ticket. Example: `To Do`, `In Progress`
GitHub Integration Integrate with GitHub to automate issue tracking and project management tasks. Provide your GitHub PAT (*personal access token*) and specify the `owner/repository`. ```bash # GitHub credentials and query settings #github_owner: "robusta-dev" #github_pat: "..." #github_url: "https://api.github.com" (default) #github_repository: "Nudge" #github_query: "is:issue is:open" ``` 1. **github_owner**: The repository owner. Eg: `robusta-dev` 2. **github_pat**: Follow these [instructions](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/managing-your-personal-access-tokens#creating-a-fine-grained-personal-access-token) to get your GitHub pat (*personal access token*). 3. **github_url**: The URL of your GitHub API. For example: [https://api.github.com](https://api.github.com) (**Note:** schema (https) is required) 4. **github_repository**: Name of the repository you want the GitHub issues to be scanned. Eg: `Nudge`.
PagerDuty Integration Integrate with PagerDuty to automate incident tracking and project management tasks. Provide your PagerDuty credentials and specify the user email to update the incident with findings. ```bash pagerduty_api_key: "..." pagerduty_user_email: "user@mail.com" pagerduty_incident_key: "..." ``` 1. **pagerduty_api_key**: The PagerDuty API key. This can be found in the PagerDuty UI under Integrations > API Access Key. 2. **pagerduty_user_email**: When --update is set, which user will be listed as the user who updated the incident. (Must be the email of a valid user in your PagerDuty account.) 3. **pagerduty_incident_key**: If provided, only analyze a single PagerDuty incident matching this key
OpsGenie Integration Integrate with OpsGenie to automate alert investigations. Provide your OpsGenie credentials and specify the query to fetch alerts. ```bash opsgenie_api_key : "..." opsgenie-team-integration-key: "...." opsgenie-query: "..." ``` 1. **opsgenie_api_key**: The OpsGenie API key. Get it from Settings > API key management > Add new API key 2. **opsgenie-team-integration-key**: OpsGenie Team Integration key for writing back results. (NOT a normal API Key.) Get it from Teams > YourTeamName > Integrations > Add Integration > API Key. Don't forget to turn on the integration and add the Team as Responders to the alert. 3. **opsgenie-query**: E.g. 'message: Foo' (see https://support.atlassian.com/opsgenie/docs/search-queries-for-alerts/)
Slack Integration Configure Slack to send notifications to specific channels. Provide your Slack token and the desired channel for notifications. ```bash # Slack token and channel configuration #slack_token: "..." #slack_channel: "#general" ``` 1. **slack-token**: The Slack API key. You can generate with `pip install robusta-cli && robusta integrations slack` 2. **slack-channel**: The Slack channel where you want to receive the findings.
Custom Runbooks Define custom runbooks to give explicit instructions to the LLM on how to investigate certain alerts. This can help in achieving better results for known alerts. ```bash # Add paths to your custom runbooks here # Example: ["path/to/your/custom_runbook.yaml"] #custom_runbooks: ["examples/custom_runbooks.yaml"] ```
### Large Language Model (LLM) Configuration Choose between OpenAI, Azure, AWS Bedrock, and more. Provide the necessary API keys and endpoints for the selected service.
OpenAI ```bash # Configuration for OpenAI LLM #api_key: "your-secret-api-key" ```
Azure ```bash # Configuration for Azure LLM #api_key: "your-secret-api-key" #model: "azure/" #you will also need to set environment variables - see above ```
Bedrock ```bash # Configuration for AWS Bedrock LLM #model: "bedrock/" #you will also need to set environment variables - see above ```
### Using Remote MCP Server Nudge can integrate with remote MCP servers using streamable-http mode through MCP Proxy (when you have multiple remote servers) or sse-mode for basic connection. #### Example MCP configuration: Data for MCP requires the following format: Connecting Nudge to proxy: ``` { "mcpServers": { "proxy": { "url": "http://127.0.0.1:8080/mcp/", "headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer " }, } } } ``` Run ```bash jq -c . | pbcopy ``` and paste it to **MCP_JSON** environment variable. Configuring Proxy: ``` { "mcpServers": { "kubernetes": { "url": "http://kubernetes-mcp-server/sse", "transport": "sse" } },... } ``` Adapt configuration in docker compose. ## License Distributed under the MIT License. See [LICENSE.txt](https://github.com/one-thd/thd-holmes/blob/master/LICENSE.txt) for more information. ## How to Contribute To contribute to Nudge, first follow the Installation instructions for **running Nudge from source using Poetry.**
Adding new runbooks You can contribute knowledge on solving common alerts and Nudge will use this knowledge to solve related issues. To do so, add a new file to [./holmes/plugins/runbooks](holmes/plugins/runbooks) - or edit an existing runbooks file in that same directory.
Adding new toolsets You can add define new tools in YAML and Nudge will use those tools in it's investigation. To do so, add a new file to [./holmes/plugins/toolsets](holmes/plugins/toolsets) - or edit an existing toolsets file in that same directory. Note: if you prefer to keep your tools private, you can store them locally and pass them to Nudge with the `-t` flag. However, please consider contributing your toolsets! At least one other community member will probably find them useful!
Modifying the default prompts (prompt engineering) The default prompts for Nudge are located in [./holmes/plugins/prompts](holmes/plugins/prompts). Most `holmes` commands accept a `--system-prompt` flag that you can use to override this. If you find a scenario where the default prompts don't work, please consider letting us know by opening a GitHub issue or messaging us on Slack! We have an internal evaluation framework for benchmarking prompts on many troubleshooting scenarios and if you share a case where Nudge doesn't work, we will be able to add it to our test framework and fix the performance on that issue and similar ones.

file.content:
[build-system]
requires = ["poetry-core"]
build-backend = "poetry.core.masonry.api"

[project]
name = "holmesgpt"
version = "0.1.0"
description = ""
authors = [{ name = "Natan Yellin", email = "natan@robusta.dev" }]
readme = "README.md"

requires-python = ">=3.12,<3.13"
dependencies = [
    "openai>=1.6.1",
    "slack-bolt>=1.18.1",
    "pydantic-settings>=2.1.0",
    "pyyaml>=6.0.1",
    "jinja2>=3.1.6",
    "typer>=0.15.2",
    "python-benedict>=0.34.1",
    "humanize>=4.9.0",
    "rich>=13.7.1",
    "pydash>=8.0.1",
    "fastapi>=0.115",
    "uvicorn>=0.34",
    "pydantic>=2.7",
    "supabase>=2.5",
    "colorlog>=6.8.2",
    "strenum>=0.4.15",
    "markdown>=3.6",
    "litellm==1.80.0",
    "certifi (>=2025.4.26,<2026.0.0)",
    "urllib3 (==2.6.3)",
    "setuptools>=78.1.1",
    "aiohttp>=3.13.3",
    "cachetools>=5.5.0",
    "starlette (>=0.49.1)",
    "backoff>=2.2.1",
    "pandas>=2.2.3",
    "google-genai>=1.5.0",
    "dnspython>=2.7.0",
    "sqlalchemy>=2.0.39",
    "html2text (>=2024.2.26,<2025.0.0)",
    "requests-cache (>=1.2.1,<2.0.0)",
    "mcp (>=1.23.0)",
    "fastmcp (>=2.14.0)",
    "sqlmodel (>=0.0.24,<0.0.25)",
    "psycopg2-binary (>=2.9.10,<3.0.0)",
    "alembic (>=1.16.2,<2.0.0)",
    "seaborn (>=0.13.2,<0.14.0)",
    "unimport (>=1.2.1,<2.0.0)",
    "plotly (==6.3.0)",
    "authlib (>=1.6.6)",
    "openlit (>=1.36.3,<2.0.0)",
    "opentelemetry-exporter-prometheus (>=0.60b1,<0.61)",
    "prometheus-client (>=0.23.1,<0.24.0)",
    "opentelemetry-instrumentation-sqlalchemy (>=0.60b1,<0.61)",
    "langchain-core (==0.3.81)",
    "pyasn1 (>=0.6.2)",
    "filelock (>=3.20.3)",
    "fonttools (>=4.61.0)",
    "python-multipart (>=0.0.22)",
    "virtualenv (>=20.36.1)",
]

[project.scripts]
holmes = "holmes.main:run"

[tool.poetry]
packages = [{ include = "holmes" }]

[tool.poetry.group.dev.dependencies]
pytest = "^8.3.3"
pytest-xdist = "^3.6.1"
ruff = "^0.7.3"
braintrust = "^0.1.0"
autoevals = "^0.0.103"
pre-commit = "^4.0.1"

[tool.poetry.scripts]
test = "scripts:__main__"

content.content:
1→# Generate timestamp in format YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS
     2→TIMESTAMP := $(shell date '+%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')
     3→# Get short Git commit SHA
     4→GIT_SHA := $(shell git rev-parse --short HEAD)
     5→# Combine timestamp and Git SHA for the tag
     6→DOCKER_TAG ?= $(GIT_SHA)-$(TIMESTAMP)
     7→
     8→DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME ?= thd-holmes
     9→DOCKER_TAG ?= latest
    10→DOCKER_REGISTRY ?= localhost
    11→DOCKER_FULL_IMAGE = $(DOCKER_REGISTRY)/$(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)
    12→DOCKER_BIN ?= docker
    13→DOCKER_FILE ?= Dockerfile
    14→TERRAFORM_DIR?=terraform
    15→TERRAFORM_VARS?="TF_PARALLELISM=40"
    16→
    17→# Default target
    18→.PHONY: all
    19→all: update-certificates build minikube-image-push minikube-image-list terraform-update-tfvars terraform-init terraform-validate terraform-auto-apply
    20→
    21→# Update terraform variables with new image tag
    22→.PHONY: terraform-update-tfvars
    23→terraform-update-tfvars:
    24→ifeq ($(shell uname),Darwin)
    25→	@sed -i '' 's|holmesgpt_image.*= ".*"|holmesgpt_image = "$(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)"|' $(TERRAFORM_DIR)/terraform.tfvars

# Generate timestamp in format YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS
TIMESTAMP := $(shell date '+%Y%m%d-%H%M%S')
# Get short Git commit SHA
GIT_SHA := $(shell git rev-parse --short HEAD)
# Combine timestamp and Git SHA for the tag
DOCKER_TAG ?= $(GIT_SHA)-$(TIMESTAMP)

DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME ?= thd-holmes
DOCKER_TAG ?= latest
DOCKER_REGISTRY ?= localhost
DOCKER_FULL_IMAGE = $(DOCKER_REGISTRY)/$(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)
DOCKER_BIN ?= docker
DOCKER_FILE ?= Dockerfile
TERRAFORM_DIR?=terraform
TERRAFORM_VARS?="TF_PARALLELISM=40"

# Default target
.PHONY: all
all: update-certificates build minikube-image-push minikube-image-list terraform-update-tfvars terraform-init terraform-validate terraform-auto-apply

# Update terraform variables with new image tag
.PHONY: terraform-update-tfvars
terraform-update-tfvars:
ifeq ($(shell uname),Darwin)
	@sed -i '' 's|holmesgpt_image.*= ".*"|holmesgpt_image = "$(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)"|' $(TERRAFORM_DIR)/terraform.tfvars
else
	@sed -i 's|holmesgpt_image.*= ".*"|holmesgpt_image   = "$(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)"|' $(TERRAFORM_DIR)/terraform.tfvars
endif
	@echo "Updated $(TERRAFORM_DIR)/terraform.tfvars with image: $(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME):$(DOCKER_TAG)"

# Docker operations
.PHONY: build
build:
	$(DOCKER_BIN) build -t $(DOCKER_FULL_IMAGE) -f $(DOCKER_FILE) .

.PHONY: minikube-image-push
minikube-image-push:
	$(DOCKER_BIN) save $(DOCKER_FULL_IMAGE) | minikube image load -

.PHONY: minikube-image-list
minikube-image-list:
	minikube image list | grep $(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME)

.PHONY: minikube-image-clean
minikube-image-clean:
	@echo "Cleaning up images for $(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME)..."
	@minikube image ls | grep $(DOCKER_IMAGE_NAME) | while read -r image; do \
	    echo "Removing $$image"; \
	    minikube image rm "$$image"; \
	done
	@echo "Image cleanup complete"


.PHONY: terraform init
terraform-init:
	cd $(TERRAFORM_DIR) && terraform init

.PHONY: terraform validate
terraform-validate:
	cd $(TERRAFORM_DIR) && terraform validate

.PHONY: terraform apply
terraform-apply:
	cd $(TERRAFORM_DIR) && terraform apply

.PHONY: terraform auto apply
terraform-auto-apply:
	export $(TERRAFORM_VARS)
	cd $(TERRAFORM_DIR) && terraform apply --auto-approve

# Minikube operations
.PHONY: minikube-start
minikube-start:
	minikube start

.PHONY: minikube-stop
minikube-stop:
	minikube stop

.PHONY: minikube-delete
minikube-delete:
	minikube delete

# Helper targets
.PHONY: status
status:
	minikube status
	$(DOCKER_BIN) info

# Combined operations
.PHONY: full-deploy
full-deploy: build push minikube-start
	@echo "Deployment complete"

.PHONY: full-cleanup
full-cleanup: minikube-stop clean
	@echo "Cleanup complete"

.PHONY: update-certificates
update-certificates:
	openssl s_client -connect slack.com:443 -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -outform PEM -out ./certs/certificate.pem
	sudo security add-trusted-cert -d -r trustRoot -k /Library/Keychains/System.keychain ./certs/certificate.pem
	security export -f pemseq -k /Library/Keychains/System.keychain -p -t certs  >"$(python3 -m certifi)"
	@echo "update-certificates complete"

.PHONY: help
help:
	@echo "Available targets:"
	@echo "  build	      - Build Docker image"
	@echo "  push	       - Push Docker image to registry"
	@echo "  minikube-start - Start Minikube cluster"
	@echo "  minikube-stop  - Stop Minikube cluster"
	@echo "  minikube-delete- Delete Minikube cluster"
	@echo "  status	     - Show Minikube and Docker status"
	@echo "  full-deploy	- Build, push and start Minikube"
	@echo "  full-cleanup   - Stop Minikube and clean Docker"

# Nudge documentation

# Introduction
Nudge is an intelligent monitoring and investigation assistant designed to process system alerts and streamline incident resolution. It integrates with existing observability tools to detect alerts, analyze potential root causes using AI and configured tools, and provide actionable insights directly in Slack.

This documentation outlines the **architecture** and **workflow** of Nudge, including its major components, interactions, and process flows. It is intended as a reference for developers, operators, and stakeholders involved in maintaining and extending the platform.

# System Overview

Nudge is an automated incident investigation service that integrates with monitoring and collaboration platforms through a plugin-based architecture. Instead of relying on a fixed set of integrations, Nudge dynamically loads and connects plugins depending on the needs of each team. This approach makes the system highly adaptable: new diagnostic tools or data sources can be added without changing the core application.

At a high level, Nudge operates as follows:

- **Input:** Alerts are received from external monitoring systems (e.g., Alertmanager) via webhook.  
- **Processing:** The system enriches alerts with metadata (e.g., Grafana dashboards, runbooks) and, upon request, initiates an AI-assisted investigation using a large language model (LLM) , which orchestrates calls to available plugins, gathers relevant evidence, and synthesizes findings into a concise incident report.
- **Output:** Results are posted to Slack in the form of enriched alerts and structured investigation summaries.  

Nudge also supports integration with **Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers**, which expose tools and data sources in a standardized way. By leveraging MCP, Nudge can connect to a wider ecosystem of observability and infrastructure services, ensuring that investigations can draw from all relevant context across the stack.

---

Nudge fits into the incident management workflow as a bridge between **alerting systems** and **engineering teams**. 
It provides context-rich notifications and on-demand investigations without requiring engineers to switch between multiple observability dashboards and systems.

### Key Benefits

- **Reduced mean time to resolution (MTTR):** Automates the repetitive first steps of incident triage.  
- **Consistent investigations:** Ensures alerts are handled with standardized procedures and tooling.  
- **Seamless integration:** Works with existing monitoring tools and Slack, minimizing disruption to workflows.  
- **Actionable context:** Provides links to dashboards, and supporting evidence directly in Slack.  


# Architecture

## Components

The Nudge system consists of the following core components:

- **Alertmanager**  
  Responsible for forwarding alerts generated by monitored applications. Based on routing rules, alerts are dispatched to the Nudge API via a secured webhook.

- **Nudge API**  
  The main entry point of the system. It receives incoming alerts from Alertmanager as well as user-triggered investigation requests from Slack. It handles authentication, normalization of alert payloads, and orchestration of downstream processing.

- **Plugin Framework**  
  Provides a standardized interface for extending Nudge. Plugins can represent external systems (e.g., Slack, PagerDuty), diagnostic tools (e.g., log search, metrics queries, Kubernetes inspections), or custom business logic. Teams can enable only the plugins that are relevant to their workflows.

- **Slack Integration Module**  
  Provides communication between Nudge and Slack. It delivers enriched alert notifications (including investigation buttons) and posts investigation results back into Slack channels or threads.

- **Nudge LLM Orchestrator**  
  Acts as a proxy between the large language model (LLM) and the configured diagnostic tools. It manages iterative prompt/response cycles with the LLM, executes tool calls (e.g., log queries, metrics retrieval, Kubernetes inspections), and consolidates results into a structured investigation output.

- **MCP Integration**  
  Provides support for Model Context Protocol servers. Through MCP, Nudge can discover and invoke external tools in a standardized way, without requiring custom plugins for each integration.

- **PostgreSQL Database**  
  Serves as the persistence layer for Nudge. It stores alerts, investigation metadata, and results, providing durability and supporting historical analysis and auditing.

## Data Flow
The Nudge data flow describes how alerts propagate through the system from initial generation to final persistence and notification.

**Alert ingestion** begins when THD applications emit alerts that are forwarded to **Alertmanager**. Alertmanager applies its configured **route matching rules** to determine the appropriate receiver. Alerts matching the Nudge configuration are sent via a **webhook** secured with **bearer token authentication**.

The **Nudge application** consumes the webhook payload and performs enrichment. During this stage, the application attaches auxiliary metadata, including:

-   a **Grafana dashboard URL** associated with the alert, and
    
-   a **Runbook URL** relevant to the affected service.
    

Following enrichment, the alert is published to **Slack**. The message contains the original alert information and an **Investigation button**, which is used to initiate the automated diagnostic workflow. The diagnostic workflow returns the completed investigation back to the slack channel

Then, Nudge writes the alert and its associated metadata to the **persistence layer (database)**.

This flow ensures that alerts are consistently authenticated, enriched with actionable context, exposed to operators through Slack, and stored for future analysis.

[view the diagrams in the Confluence page](https://thd.atlassian.net/wiki/x/cQCBpg)
![alert to slack notification diagram](/images/alert_notification_diagram.png)


## Workflow
The Nudge investigation workflow defines the sequence of operations triggered when a Slack user initiates an investigation.

The process begins when the **Investigation button** is pressed in Slack. The action payload is forwarded to Nudge, which instantiates an **Issue Investigator** and renders the corresponding **user prompt**. This prompt is then supplied to the **large language model (LLM)** for reasoning.

Nudge implements a controlled execution loop around the LLM. At each iteration:

-   The LLM response is evaluated.

- If the maximum number of iterations has been reached, Nudge forces LLM to return a response instead of a tool call  

-   If no tool calls are present, the system finalizes the investigation and returns results.
    
-   If tool calls are present, Nudge validates the tool name and parameters. Invalid calls are skipped; valid calls trigger execution.
    
-   If token limits are exceeded, the system truncates responses before re-submitting to the LLM.
    

When tools are invoked, the following integrations are supported:

-   **Grafana Loki** – retrieval of pod logs.
    
-   **Grafana Tempo** – retrieval of pod traces.
    
-   **Grafana Prometheus** – collection of replica counts and metrics.
    
-   **Kubernetes MCP** – inspection of cluster resources.

- **CUJs (Critical User Journeys)** – encapsulated diagnostic workflows that reflect expected user interactions and system paths. These serve as higher-level investigative tools.  

- **Argus Rules** – predefined rulesets that detect known failure patterns. They act as automated checks to quickly highlight potential root causes.  

    

Each tool response is parsed, deduplicated, and converted into a **StructuredToolResult**. The results are then aggregated and passed through a post-processing step, which consolidates evidence and formats the investigation output.

The workflow concludes by returning the investigation results to Slack. The output contains a summarized diagnostic report along with references to logs, traces, metrics, and dashboards.

[view the diagrams in the Confluence page](https://thd.atlassian.net/wiki/x/cQCBpg)
![investigation diagram](/images/investigation_diagram.png)

## Security & Authentication

Nudge integrates with external systems (Slack, Alertmanager) that require authentication to ensure secure 
communication and prevent unauthorized access. The following mechanisms are implemented:

### Slack Authentication
Nudge relies on the standard Slack authentication mechanism provided by the Slack API. No custom logic is implemented beyond the upstream workflow.  

The authentication check in Nudge is primarily used as a **health probe**: it verifies that the Slack plugin is correctly connected to the target workspace and that the bot identity is valid. This ensures that messages, alerts, and investigation results can be delivered without interruption.  

Because authentication is handled through Slack’s native API, security and token management follow Slack’s recommended practices. The Nudge implementation does not introduce additional layers on top of Slack’s existing model.

### Alertmanager Authentication

Alerts are delivered from Alertmanager to the Nudge API via a webhook endpoint. Access to this endpoint is protected using a bearer token. The token is not stored in code but managed as a Kubernetes Secret, which is then referenced by the `AlertmanagerConfig`. Any request to the Nudge API that does not include the correct token is rejected. This mechanism ensures that alerts are only accepted from trusted Alertmanager instances.

### Secret Management

Nudge relies on Google Secret Manager (GSM) for storing sensitive credentials, including the Slack bot token and the Alertmanager bearer token. Secrets are never embedded in configuration files or Kubernetes manifests. Instead, they are securely managed in GSM and synchronized into the cluster at runtime using the External Secrets Operator (ESO).

This approach ensures:
- Centralized management of secrets across environments.  
- Secure rotation of tokens without requiring application redeployment.  
- Fine-grained access control, since only the ESO service account is permitted to retrieve secrets from GSM.  
- Automatic propagation of updated secrets into the Kubernetes runtime.  

By leveraging GSM and ESO, Nudge avoids the pitfalls of static Kubernetes Secrets while still ensuring that credentials are securely available to the components that need them, following the principle of least privilege.

    77→    @classmethod
    78→    def load_from_env(cls):
    79→        kwargs = {}
    80→        for field_name in [
    81→            "model",
    82→            "api_key",
    83→            "max_steps",
    84→            "alertmanager_url",
    85→            "alertmanager_username",
    86→            "alertmanager_password",

   100→            "allowed_toolsets",
   101→            # TODO
   102→            # custom_runbooks
   103→            # custom_toolsets
   104→        ]:
   105→            val = os.getenv(field_name.upper(), None)
   106→            if val is not None:
   107→                kwargs[field_name] = val
   108→        return cls(**kwargs)
   109→
   110→    def create_tool_executor(
   111→        self,
   112→        console: Console,
   113→        dal: Optional[SupabaseDal],
   114→        allowed_toolsets: ToolsetPattern = None,
   115→    ) -> ToolExecutor:
   116→        if not allowed_toolsets:
   117→            allowed_toolsets = self.allowed_toolsets
   118→        all_toolsets = load_builtin_toolsets(dal=dal)
   119→        for ts_path in self.custom_toolsets:
   120→            all_toolsets.extend(load_toolsets_from_file_helper(ts_path))
   121→
   122→        if os.path.isfile(CUSTOM_TOOLSET_LOCATION):
   123→            try:
   124→                all_toolsets.extend(
   125→                    load_toolsets_from_file_helper(CUSTOM_TOOLSET_LOCATION)
   126→                )
   127→            except Exception as error:
   128→                logging.error(
   129→                    f"An error happened while trying to use custom toolset: {error}"
   130→                )
   131→
   132→        if allowed_toolsets == "*":
   133→            matching_toolsets = all_toolsets
   134→        else:
   135→            matching_toolsets = get_matching_toolsets(
   136→                all_toolsets, allowed_toolsets.split(",")
   137→            )
   138→
   139→        enabled_toolsets = [ts for ts in matching_toolsets if ts.is_enabled()]
   140→        logging.info(f"All toolsets: {[ts.name for ts in all_toolsets]}")
   141→        logging.info(f"Enabled toolsets: {[ts.name for ts in enabled_toolsets]}")
   142→        logging.info(f"Allowed toolsets: {allowed_toolsets}")
   143→        for ts in all_toolsets:
   144→            if ts not in matching_toolsets:
   145→                logging.debug(f"Disabling toolset {ts.name} from {ts.get_path()})")
   146→            elif ts not in enabled_toolsets:
   147→                logging.warning(
   148→                    f"Not loading toolset {ts.name} ({ts.get_disabled_reason()})"
   149→                )
   150→            else:
   151→                logging.debug(f"{ts.name} toolset is loaded")
   152→
   153→        enabled_tools = concat(*[ts.tools for ts in enabled_toolsets])
   154→        logging.info(
   155→            f"Starting AI session with tools: {[t.name for t in enabled_tools]}"
   156→        )
   157→        return ToolExecutor(enabled_toolsets)

   164→    ) -> ToolCallingLLM:
   165→        tool_executor = self.create_tool_executor(console, dal, allowed_toolsets)
   166→        return ToolCallingLLM(tool_executor, self.max_steps, self._get_llm())

   179→        )

   255→    def create_slack_destination(self, slack=None):
   256→        if self.slack_token is None:
   257→            raise ValueError("--slack-token must be specified")
   258→        if self.slack_app_token is None:
   259→            raise ValueError("--slack-app-token must be specified")
   260→        if self.slack_channel is None:
   261→            raise ValueError("--slack-channel must be specified")
   262→        if self.slack_channel_id is None:
   263→            raise ValueError("--slack-channel-id must be specified")
   264→        if not slack:
   265→            slack = SlackDestination(
   266→                web_token=self.slack_token.get_secret_value(),
   267→                app_token=self.slack_app_token.get_secret_value(),
   268→                channel=self.slack_channel,
   269→                channel_id=self.slack_channel_id,
   270→            )
   271→        return slack
   272→
   273→    @classmethod
   274→    def load_from_file(cls, config_file: Optional[str], **kwargs) -> "Config":
   275→        if config_file is not None:
   276→            logging.info(f"Loading config from file %s", config_file)
   277→            config_from_file = load_model_from_file(cls, config_file)
   278→        elif os.path.exists(DEFAULT_CONFIG_LOCATION):
   279→            logging.info(
   280→                f"Loading config from default location {DEFAULT_CONFIG_LOCATION}"
   281→            )
   282→            config_from_file = load_model_from_file(cls, DEFAULT_CONFIG_LOCATION)
   283→        else:
   284→            logging.warning(
   285→                f"No config file found at {DEFAULT_CONFIG_LOCATION}, using cli settings only"
   286→            )
   287→            config_from_file = None
   288→
   289→        cli_options = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items() if v is not None and v != []}
   290→        if config_from_file is None:
   291→            return cls(**cli_options)
   292→
   293→        merged_config = config_from_file.dict()
   294→        merged_config.update(cli_options)
   295→        return cls(**merged_config)
   296→
   297→    def _get_llm(self) -> LLM:
   298→        api_key = self.api_key.get_secret_value() if self.api_key else None
   299→        return DefaultLLM(self.model, api_key)

    55→class Verbosity(Enum):
    56→    NORMAL = 0
    57→    LOG_QUERIES = 1  # TODO: currently unused
    58→    VERBOSE = 2
    59→    VERY_VERBOSE = 3
    60→
    61→
    62→def cli_flags_to_verbosity(verbose_flags: List[bool]) -> Verbosity:
    63→    if verbose_flags is None or len(verbose_flags) == 0:
    64→        return Verbosity.NORMAL
    65→    elif len(verbose_flags) == 1:
    66→        return Verbosity.LOG_QUERIES
    67→    elif len(verbose_flags) == 2:
    68→        return Verbosity.VERBOSE
    69→    else:
    70→        return Verbosity.VERY_VERBOSE
    71→
    72→
    73→def suppress_noisy_logs():
    74→    # disable INFO logs from OpenAI
    75→    # Disable httpx logging completely for security (prevents API key exposure in tracebacks)
    76→    httpx_logger = logging.getLogger("httpx")
    77→    httpx_logger.setLevel(logging.CRITICAL)
    78→    httpx_logger.disabled = True
    79→    # disable INFO logs from LiteLLM
    80→    logging.getLogger("LiteLLM").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
    81→    logging.getLogger("LiteLLM Router").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
    82→    logging.getLogger("LiteLLM Proxy").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
    83→    # disable INFO logs from AWS (relevant when using bedrock)
    84→    logging.getLogger("boto3").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
    85→    logging.getLogger("botocore").setLevel(logging.WARNING)
    86→    # when running in --verbose mode we don't want to see DEBUG logs from these libraries
    87→    logging.getLogger("openai._base_client").setLevel(logging.INFO)
    88→    logging.getLogger("httpcore").setLevel(logging.INFO)
    89→    logging.getLogger("markdown_it").setLevel(logging.INFO)
    90→    # suppress UserWarnings from the slack_sdk module
    91→    warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=UserWarning, module="slack_sdk.*")
    92→
    93→
    94→def init_logging(verbose_flags: List[bool] = None):
    95→    verbosity = cli_flags_to_verbosity(verbose_flags)
    96→
    97→    if verbosity == Verbosity.VERY_VERBOSE:
    98→        logging.basicConfig(
    99→            level=logging.DEBUG,
   100→            format="%(message)s",
   101→            handlers=[RichHandler(show_level=False, show_time=False)],
   102→        )
   103→    elif verbosity == Verbosity.VERBOSE:
   104→        logging.basicConfig(
   105→            level=logging.INFO,
   106→            format="%(message)s",
   107→            handlers=[RichHandler(show_level=False, show_time=False)],
   108→        )
   109→        logging.getLogger().setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
   110→        suppress_noisy_logs()
   111→    else:
   112→        logging.basicConfig(
   113→            level=logging.INFO,
   114→            format="%(message)s",
   115→            handlers=[RichHandler(show_level=False, show_time=False)],
   116→        )
   117→        suppress_noisy_logs()
   118→
   119→    logging.debug(f"verbosity is {verbosity}")
   120→
   121→    return Console()

   207→def parse_documents(documents: Optional[str]) -> List[ResourceInstructionDocument]:
   208→    resource_documents = []
   209→
   210→    if documents is not None:
   211→        data = json.loads(documents)
   212→        for item in data:
   213→            resource_document = ResourceInstructionDocument(**item)
   214→            resource_documents.append(resource_document)
   215→
   216→    return resource_documents

   219→def handle_result(
   220→    console: Console,
   221→    destination: DestinationType,
   222→    config: Config,
   223→    issue: Issue,
   224→    show_tool_output: bool,
   225→    add_separator: bool,
   226→    result: Optional[LLMResult] = None,
   227→    slack: SlackDestination = None,
   228→):
   229→    if destination == DestinationType.CLI:
   230→        if show_tool_output and result.tool_calls:
   231→            for tool_call in result.tool_calls:
   232→                console.print(f"[bold magenta]Used Tool:[/bold magenta]", end="")
   233→                # we need to print this separately with markup=False because it contains arbitrary text and we don't want console.print to interpret it
   234→                console.print(
   235→                    f"{tool_call.description}. Output=\n{tool_call.result}",
   236→                    markup=False,
   237→                )
   238→
   239→        console.print(f"[bold green]AI:[/bold green]", end=" ")
   240→        console.print(Markdown(result.result))
   241→        if add_separator:
   242→            console.print(Rule())
   243→
   244→    elif destination == DestinationType.SLACK:
   245→        slack = config.create_slack_destination(slack=slack)
   246→        slack.send_issue(issue, result)
